devanagari: गोकुले भगवतो जातकर्मादि महोत्सवः । नन्दस्य मथुरागमनं तत्र नन्दवसुदेवसंवादः । श्रीशुक उवाच । नन्दस्त्वात्मज उत्पन्ने जाताह्लादो महामनाः ।...
english_devnagari: gokule bhagavato jātakarmādi mahotsavaḥnandasya mathurāgamanaṃ tatra nandavasudevasaṃvādaḥśrīśuka uvācanandastvātmaja utpanne jātāhlādo mahāmanāḥāhūya viprān vedajñān snātaḥ śuciralaṃkṛtaḥ
translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
Delighted at the birth of a son, the noble-minded Nanda too took a bath, got purified and adorned himself. He sent for Brāhmaṇas well versed in astrology and made them recite the Svastyayana (prescribed benedictory Vedic hymns) and got the jāta-karma ceremony of his child, as well as the worship of his forebears (pitṛs) and gods duly performed according to precepts.

devanagari: वाचयित्वा स्वस्त्ययनं जातकर्मात्मजस्य वै । कारयामास विधिवत् पितृदेवार्चनं तथा ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: vācayitvā svastyayanaṃ jātakarmātmajasya vaikārayāmāsa vidhivat pitṛdevārcanaṃ tathā
translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
Delighted at the birth of a son, the noble-minded Nanda too took a bath, got purified and adorned himself. He sent for Brāhmaṇas well versed in astrology and made them recite the Svastyayana (prescribed benedictory Vedic hymns) and got the jāta-karma ceremony of his child, as well as the worship of his forebears (pitṛs) and gods duly performed according to precepts.

devanagari: धेनूनां नियुते प्रादाद् विप्रेभ्यः समलंकृते । तिलाद्रीन् सप्त रत्‍नौघ शातकौंभांबरावृतान् ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: dhenūnāṃ niyute prādād viprebhyaḥ samalaṃkṛtetilādrīn sapta rat‍naugha śātakauṃbhāṃbarāvṛtān
translation: As a gift (on this joyous occasion), he presented to Brāhmaṇas two-hundred thousands of richly adorned cows, and seven mountain-like heaps[1] of sesamum seeds covered with streams (large quantities) of precious stones and gold-cloth.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
VC. quotes the Bhaviṣyottara Purāṇa and states the best ‘Sesamum mountain’ consists of ten droṇas, medium of five, and the lowest of three droṇas.

devanagari: कालेन स्नानशौचाभ्यां संस्कारैः तपसेज्यया । शुध्यन्ति दानैः सन्तुष्ट्या द्रव्याण्यात्माऽत्मविद्यया ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: kālena snānaśaucābhyāṃ saṃskāraiḥ tapasejyayāśudhyanti dānaiḥ santuṣṭyā dravyāṇyātmā'tmavidyayā
translation: [2] Some material things become purified by passage of time (e.g. the earth), some by ablution (e.g. our body), or washing (e.g. things covered with dirt), some by special rites (e.g. the foetus), some by penance (e.g. senses), some by sacrificial performance (e.g. Brāhmaṇas), some by charitable gifts (wealth), some by contentment (mind), while the soul is purified by (meditation and) knowledge of the Self.
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Examples in the bracket are supplied by Bhāvāratha Dīpikā

devanagari: सौमंगल्यगिरो विप्राः सूतमागधवन्दिनः । गायकाश्च जगुर्नेदुः भेर्यो दुन्दुभयो मुहुः ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: saumaṃgalyagiro viprāḥ sūtamāgadhavandinaḥgāyakāśca jagurneduḥ bheryo dundubhayo muhuḥ
translation: Brāhmaṇas, Sūtas[3], Māgadhas and Vandins uttered auspicious blessings; musicians sang; drums and kettle-drums were frequently sounded.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
Normally sūtas are the reciters of Purāṇas; Māgadhas are bards reciting the glories of royal dynasties; Vandins are clearheaded persons making observations suitable for the occasion.
sūtāḥ paurāṇikāḥ proktā, māgadhā vaṃśa-śaṃsakāḥ /
vandinas tvamala-prajñāḥ prastāva-sadṛśoktayaḥ //
But Padaratnāvalī states: Sūtas sing the eulogies composed by themselves, Māgadhas sing compositions of others, while Vandins read panygerics from books.

devanagari: व्रजः सम्मृष्टसंसिक्त द्वाराजिरगृहान्तरः । चित्रध्वज पताकास्रक् चैलपल्लवतोरणैः ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: vrajaḥ sammṛṣṭasaṃsikta dvārājiragṛhāntaraḥcitradhvaja patākāsrak cailapallavatoraṇaiḥ
translation: All the entrances, enclosures, and interiors of the houses of Vraja were well-swept and properly besprinkled (with water). It (the Vraja) was decorated with flags and buntings of different colours, banners, wreaths, arches of (silken) raiments, and tender leaves.

devanagari: गावो वृषा वत्सतरा हरिद्रातैलरूषिताः । विचित्र धातुबर्हस्रग् वस्त्रकाञ्चनमालिनः ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: gāvo vṛṣā vatsatarā haridrātailarūṣitāḥvicitra dhātubarhasrag vastrakāñcanamālinaḥ
translation: The oxen, cows and heifers (grown up calves) were smeared with oil mixed with turmeric power. They were painted with various colours, and adorned with peacock feathers, wreaths, cloths and gold chains (round the neck).

devanagari: महार्हवस्त्राभरण कञ्चुकोष्णीषभूषिताः । गोपाः समाययू राजन् नानोपायनपाणयः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: mahārhavastrābharaṇa kañcukoṣṇīṣabhūṣitāḥgopāḥ samāyayū rājan nānopāyanapāṇayaḥ
translation: Adorned with valuable garments, costly ornaments, coats and turbans, the cowherds came to visit (Nanda) with various kinds of presents in their hands.

devanagari: गोप्यश्चाकर्ण्य मुदिता यशोदायाः सुतोद्‍भवम् । आत्मानं भूषयां चक्रुः वस्त्राकल्पाञ्जनादिभिः ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: gopyaścākarṇya muditā yaśodāyāḥ sutod‍bhavamātmānaṃ bhūṣayāṃ cakruḥ vastrākalpāñjanādibhiḥ
translation: On hearing the news of the birth of a son to Yaśodā, the cowherd women were delighted, and they beautified themselves with costly garments, ornaments and (the make-up of) collyrium etc.

devanagari: नवकुंकुमकिञ्जल्क मुखपंकजभूतयः । बलिभिस्त्वरितं जग्मुः पृथुश्रोण्यश्चलत्कुचाः ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: navakuṃkumakiñjalka mukhapaṃkajabhūtayaḥbalibhistvaritaṃ jagmuḥ pṛthuśroṇyaścalatkucāḥ
translation: The beauty of their lotus-like faces was enhanced with the decoration of saffron-paste like unto filaments heightening the beauty of a lotus. In spite of their heavy buttocks, they hastily went with presents (to Yaśodā) heaving their heavy breasts (while going).

devanagari: गोप्यः सुमृष्टमणिकुण्डल निष्ककण्ठ्यः । चित्राम्बराः पथि शिखाच्युतमाल्यवर्षाः । नन्दालयं सवलया व्रजतीर्विरेजुः । व्यालोलकुण्डल पयोधरहारश...
english_devnagari: gopyaḥ sumṛṣṭamaṇikuṇḍala niṣkakaṇṭhyaḥcitrāmbarāḥ pathi śikhācyutamālyavarṣāḥnandālayaṃ savalayā vrajatīrvirejuḥvyālolakuṇḍala payodharahāraśobhāḥ
translation: Wearing ear-rings set with well-polished jewels, necklaces of gold-coins about their necks, and clad in wonderfully wrought garments of various colours the gopa damsels proceeded to the palace of Nanda showering on the way flowers dropped from the braids of their hair. They appeared (lit. shone) most beautiful with bangles round their wrists, their swaying ear-rings and dangling necklaces on heaving breasts.

devanagari: ता आशिषः प्रयुञ्जानाः चिरं पाहीति बालके । हरिद्राचूर्णतैलाद्भिः सिञ्चन्त्यो जनमुज्जगुः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: tā āśiṣaḥ prayuñjānāḥ ciraṃ pāhīti bālakeharidrācūrṇatailādbhiḥ siñcantyo janamujjaguḥ
translation: Invoking blessings (of God) on the child, “May you protect the child for a long time”[4] they sprayed the (assembled) people with water mixed with oil and turmeric power and sang lustily. (Or: they sang in a high pitch the praise of the Unborn—Kṛṣṇa).
purport:
Footnote [4]:
Or: May you protect us forever—Bhāgavata Candrikā and others.
This interpretation is obviously illogical and out of context with the sentence, “Invoking blessings on the child”.

devanagari: अवाद्यन्त विचित्राणि वादित्राणि महोत्सवे । कृष्णे विश्वेश्वरेऽनन्ते नंदस्य व्रजमागते ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: avādyanta vicitrāṇi vāditrāṇi mahotsavekṛṣṇe viśveśvare'nante naṃdasya vrajamāgate
translation: On the great festive occasion of the coming of Lord Kṛṣṇa, the Infinite, the Supreme Lord of the universe, a variety of musical instruments were played on.

devanagari: गोपाः परस्परं हृष्टा दधिक्षीरघृतांबुभिः । आसिञ्चन्तो विलिंपन्तो नवनीतैश्च चिक्षिपुः ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: gopāḥ parasparaṃ hṛṣṭā dadhikṣīraghṛtāṃbubhiḥāsiñcanto viliṃpanto navanītaiśca cikṣipuḥ
translation: The merry cowherds joyously sprayed and besmeared one another with curds, milk, ghee and water and they threw (balls of) butter at each other.[5]
purport:
Footnote [5]:
Or: Thickly besmearing one another with butter, they tried to throw each other on (the slippery) ground.—VT.

devanagari: नन्दो महामनास्तेभ्यो वासोऽलंकारगोधनम् । सूतमागधवन्दिभ्यो येऽन्ये विद्योपजीविनः ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: nando mahāmanāstebhyo vāso'laṃkāragodhanamsūtamāgadhavandibhyo ye'nye vidyopajīvinaḥ
translation: [6] For the propitiation of Lord Viṣṇu and for the well-being of his son, the noble-minded Nanda presented to the cowherds garments, ornaments and wealth in the form of cows (or kine, gold and silver). The generous-hearted Nanda respectfully worshipped Sūtas, Māgadhas, Vandins and others who lived by their arts, skills or crafts (e.g. astrologers, dancers etc.), and conferred upon them their (respective) desired objects.
purport:
Footnote [6]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā: The noble-minded Nanda presented to cowherds as also to Sūtas, Māgadhas, Vandins and others who lived by their skill in arts, crafts, etc., garments, ornaments, kine and wealth.
For the propitiation of Lord Viṣṇu and the well-being of the child, the generous-minded Nanda honoured others (visitors, guests etc.) by bestowing on them their desired objects.

devanagari: तैस्तैः कामैरदीनात्मा यथोचितं अपूजयत् । विष्णोराराधनार्थाय स्वपुत्रस्योदयाय च ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: taistaiḥ kāmairadīnātmā yathocitaṃ apūjayatviṣṇorārādhanārthāya svaputrasyodayāya ca
translation: [6] For the propitiation of Lord Viṣṇu and for the well-being of his son, the noble-minded Nanda presented to the cowherds garments, ornaments and wealth in the form of cows (or kine, gold and silver). The generous-hearted Nanda respectfully worshipped Sūtas, Māgadhas, Vandins and others who lived by their arts, skills or crafts (e.g. astrologers, dancers etc.), and conferred upon them their (respective) desired objects.
purport:
Footnote [6]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā: The noble-minded Nanda presented to cowherds as also to Sūtas, Māgadhas, Vandins and others who lived by their skill in arts, crafts, etc., garments, ornaments, kine and wealth.
For the propitiation of Lord Viṣṇu and the well-being of the child, the generous-minded Nanda honoured others (visitors, guests etc.) by bestowing on them their desired objects.

devanagari: रोहिणी च महाभागा नंदगोपाभिनंदिता । व्यचरद् दिव्यवासःस्रक् कण्ठाभरणभूषिता ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: rohiṇī ca mahābhāgā naṃdagopābhinaṃditāvyacarad divyavāsaḥsrak kaṇṭhābharaṇabhūṣitā
translation: Welcomed by Nanda, the chief of cowherds, Rohiṇī, who was highly fortunate (as she was to be witness to the sportive activities of Lord Kṛṣṇa from his childhood) was adorned with very costly (heavenly-type) Lower garment and other clothes, garland and necklace and was moving about in Nanda’s palace (particularly in that reception).

devanagari: तत आरभ्य नंदस्य व्रजः सर्वसमृद्धिमान् । हरेर्निवासात्मगुणै रमाक्रीडमभून् नृप ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: tata ārabhya naṃdasya vrajaḥ sarvasamṛddhimānharernivāsātmaguṇai ramākrīḍamabhūn nṛpa
translation: Thence forward the Vraja of Nanda became blessed with prosperity in every respect as it was turned into a pleasure garden for the sporting of goddess Lakṣmī due to its becoming the abode of Hari, Oh King.

devanagari: गोपान् गोकुलरक्षायां निरूप्य मथुरां गतः । नंदः कंसस्य वार्षिक्यं करं दातुं कुरूद्वह ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: gopān gokularakṣāyāṃ nirūpya mathurāṃ gataḥnaṃdaḥ kaṃsasya vārṣikyaṃ karaṃ dātuṃ kurūdvaha
translation: Having instructed the cowherds to take care of and protect the Gokula, Nanda went to Mathurā to pay his annual tribute to Kaṃsa, Oh Parīkṣit (the best of Kurus).

devanagari: वसुदेव उपश्रुत्य भ्रातरं नंदमागतम् । ज्ञात्वा दत्तकरं राज्ञे ययौ तद् अवमोचनम् ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: vasudeva upaśrutya bhrātaraṃ naṃdamāgatamjñātvā dattakaraṃ rājñe yayau tad avamocanam
translation: Having learnt (from the public) that his brotherly Nanda had come and having (reliably) known that Nanda’s (official) work of payment of tributes was over, Vasudeva repaired to his lodging place (the parking ground of wagons).

devanagari: तं दृष्ट्वा सहसोत्थाय देहः प्राणमिवागतम् । प्रीतः प्रियतमं दोर्भ्यां सस्वजे प्रेमविह्वलः ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ dṛṣṭvā sahasotthāya dehaḥ prāṇamivāgatamprītaḥ priyatamaṃ dorbhyāṃ sasvaje premavihvalaḥ
translation: Seeing him (Vasudeva) come, he promptly sprang to his feet as a body (in an unconscious stage) does on the return of life (or consciousness). Being overjoyed and overwhelmed with affection, he flung his arms round his dearest friend to embrace him.

devanagari: पूजितः सुखमासीनः पृष्ट्वा अनामयमादृतः । प्रसक्तधीः स्वात्मजयोः इदमाह विशांपते ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: pūjitaḥ sukhamāsīnaḥ pṛṣṭvā anāmayamādṛtaḥprasaktadhīḥ svātmajayoḥ idamāha viśāṃpate
translation: Oh Protector of the People! When Vasudeva was respectfully received, comfortably seated and was honoured with the enquiry about his health, he, with his mind deeply attached to both of his sons, courteously addressed Nanda as follows:

devanagari: दिष्ट्या भ्रातः प्रवयस इदानीमप्रजस्य ते । प्रजाशाया निवृत्तस्य प्रजा यत् समपद्यत ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: diṣṭyā bhrātaḥ pravayasa idānīmaprajasya teprajāśāyā nivṛttasya prajā yat samapadyata
translation: “Brother! It is indeed fortunate that a darling child has been born to you who were childless till this advanced age and had lost hopes of getting an issue.

devanagari: दिष्ट्या संसारचक्रेऽस्मिन् वर्तमानः पुनर्भवः । उपलब्धो भवानद्य दुर्लभं प्रियदर्शनम् ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: diṣṭyā saṃsāracakre'smin vartamānaḥ punarbhavaḥupalabdho bhavānadya durlabhaṃ priyadarśanam
translation: It is due to sheer good luck that you have met me today like one born again in this whirling cycle of Saṃsāra. For the sight of dear ones is very rare.

devanagari: नैकत्र प्रियसंवासः सुहृदां चित्रकर्मणाम् । ओघेन व्यूह्यमानानां प्लवानां स्रोतसो यथा ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: naikatra priyasaṃvāsaḥ suhṛdāṃ citrakarmaṇāmoghena vyūhyamānānāṃ plavānāṃ srotaso yathā
translation: Oh dear friend! The staying together of intimate friends (howsoever dear but) following varied professions, is not possible like unto the coming together of floating logs of wood (etc.) in a stream of water but which are swept away by the current.

devanagari: कच्चित् पशव्यं निरुजं भूर्यम्बुतृणवीरुधम् । बृहद्वनं तदधुना यत्रास्से त्वं सुहृद्वृतः ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: kaccit paśavyaṃ nirujaṃ bhūryambutṛṇavīrudhambṛhadvanaṃ tadadhunā yatrāsse tvaṃ suhṛdvṛtaḥ
translation: Is the Gokula (Vṛndāvana) where you are now dwelling accompanied (lit. surrounded) by your friends and relatives favourable to the cattle? Does it abound in plenty of water, grass and plants? Is it free from disease and epidemics?

devanagari: भ्रातर्मम सुतः कच्चित् मात्रा सह भवद्व्रजे । तातं भवन्तं मन्वानो भवद्‍भ्यामुपलालितः ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: bhrātarmama sutaḥ kaccit mātrā saha bhavadvrajetātaṃ bhavantaṃ manvāno bhavad‍bhyāmupalālitaḥ
translation: Oh brother! Does my son (Balarāma) who regards you to be his father and is dearly fondled by you and your wife, doing well along with his mother, in your Vraja?

devanagari: पुंसस्त्रिवर्गो विहितः सुहृदो ह्यनुभावितः । न तेषु क्लिश्यमानेषु त्रिवर्गोऽर्थाय कल्पते ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: puṃsastrivargo vihitaḥ suhṛdo hyanubhāvitaḥna teṣu kliśyamāneṣu trivargo'rthāya kalpate
translation: For, the threefold objective of human life (viz. dharma, artha and kāma) is prescribed (as beneficial) insofar as they are shared with one’s kith and kin. But if they suffer distress, that threefold purpose of life does not contribute to happiness.”

devanagari: श्रीनंद उवाच । अहो ते देवकी पुत्राः कंसेन बहवो हताः । एकावशिष्टावरजा कन्या सापि दिवं गता ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīnaṃda uvācaaho te devakī putrāḥ kaṃsena bahavo hatāḥekāvaśiṣṭāvarajā kanyā sāpi divaṃ gatā
translation:
Nanda said:
“Alas! Many of your sons born of Devakī have been killed by Kaṃsa. And only the youngest daughter survived—even she flew to heaven.

devanagari: नूनं ह्यदृष्टनिष्ठोऽयं अदृष्टपरमो जनः । अदृष्टमात्मनस्तत्त्वं यो वेद न स मुह्यति ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: nūnaṃ hyadṛṣṭaniṣṭho'yaṃ adṛṣṭaparamo janaḥadṛṣṭamātmanastattvaṃ yo veda na sa muhyati
translation: [7] It is definite that people in this world have their end in the unseen (according to their destiny. When good fortune, blessing one with the pleasure of having sons etc., ceases to smile, one is bereaved of them). The destiny is supreme. (If one is separated from one’s sons, the destiny reunites them). He who knows that the destiny is the reality about Atman (and as such is the ever constant cause of pleasure and pain) does not get bewildered.”
purport:
Footnote [7]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā: All people definitely find their end in the unseen (as destined along with happiness and misery). The destiny is the ultimate supreme controller. He who realises that pleasure and pain are always due to his destiny, is always equable and serene in prosperity and adversity.

devanagari: श्रीवसुदेव उवाच । करो वै वार्षिको दत्तो राज्ञे दृष्टा वयं च वः । नेह स्थेयं बहुतिथं सन्त्युत्पाताश्च गोकुले ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīvasudeva uvācakaro vai vārṣiko datto rājñe dṛṣṭā vayaṃ ca vaḥneha stheyaṃ bahutithaṃ santyutpātāśca gokule
translation:
Vasudeva said:
“The annual tribute has been paid by you to the king, and we have seen (each other). You should not stay here any longer, for omens indicating calamities are seen at Gokula.”

devanagari: श्रीशुक उवाच । इति नंदादयो गोपाः प्रोक्तास्ते शौरिणा ययुः । अनोभिः अनडुद्युक्तैः तं अनुज्ञाप्य गोकुलम् ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīśuka uvācaiti naṃdādayo gopāḥ proktāste śauriṇā yayuḥanobhiḥ anaḍudyuktaiḥ taṃ anujñāpya gokulam
translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
Thus advised by Vasudeva, Nanda and other cowherds, taking leave of him, mounted on carts drawn by bullocks and returned to Gokula.
